arguments_against: The strongest counter-hypothesis is a same-name son or relative: the bio's appointee of 1872 could have retired in the 1880s and been succeeded in the same surveyorship by a son sharing the initials A. W. H., which would make the 1890-1898 Antigua stint and the 1897-1899 Leeward Islands stint a different man. This fails for several reasons. The biography pins this exact, specialized post to this man ('Surveyor of public works, Antigua, 8 April, 1872'), and the staff lists show one continuous incumbent of the identical post ('Surveyor of Public Works, Roads, &c.') under the identical three initials from 1883 through 1899 with no break, no overlap, and no second A. W. H. A'Court ever listed simultaneously — a father/son pair sharing all three initials and the same specialized office seamlessly is far less parsimonious than one career. The 'Hon.' prefix on the bio is matched in the staff lists (1883 and 1898) and is explained by the 1897 'Legislative Council (Local)' membership, tying the stints together as one person. The mechanical colony FAILs on the two Leeward Islands stints are an artifact: Antigua was a presidency of the Leeward Islands federation, so the same Antigua establishment was printed under the federation heading in some editions; the position string is verbatim identical across all three stints. A second trap — honoured retiree kept on the list — does not apply because the listings carry an active Civil Establishment post, not a courtesy entry. Corpus context (only 4 officials with this rare surname) further limits the homonym pool, and the only differently-initialed form ('H') has been left unassigned.

evidence_cited.fact: Surveyor of public works, Antigua, 8 April, 1872.

would_overturn: A printed birth/death year or obituary showing the 1872 appointee died or left the service before 1890; a second biography or staff-list entry showing two distinct A. W. H. A'Courts listed simultaneously in different posts; or evidence that the Antigua surveyorship passed to a son of the same initials (e.g., a Record of Services entry dating a NEW appointment to the post in the 1880s-90s).

A'Court' stint is very plausibly the same man (the bio dates his appointment to this exact post to 1872, before the stint begins) and is the natural start of the same continuous 1877-1899 career; recommend a human reviewer check the second A'Court biography in the corpus before linking it.

arguments_against: The strongest counter-hypothesis is a same-name relative: Abayomi-Cole is a known Sierra Leone Creole family name, and a father, brother, or cousin with the same 'A. B.' initials could have served as a Medical Officer in the same colony. The deterministic edition_cooccurrence check also fails (stint in 1949-1951 editions, biography only in 1956-1963 editions), which could suggest two different people whose listings never coincide. This fails for several reasons. First, the corpus context shows exactly 1 official and 1 biography with this surname across the entire graph — there is no second Abayomi-Cole anywhere in the staff lists or biographical sections to be confused with. Second, the biography's own career clause states he was appointed 'M.O., S.L., 1944' and was not promoted to S.M.O. until 1955, which means the biography itself asserts he was serving as a Medical Officer in Sierra Leone throughout 1949-1951 — exactly what the stint records. The position_sim FAIL is a pure abbreviation artifact ('M.O.' vs 'Medical Officer'); read as text the positions are identical. Third, the edition gap is explained mechanically: the biography simply does not appear in editions before 1956, so no co-occurrence was possible; the stint window (1949-1951) sits squarely inside the bio's attested M.O. tenure (1944-1955). Fourth, this is a two-initial match ('A. B.'), not the single-initial trap, and the age gate passes comfortably (age 42 at stint start, mid-career, consistent with a 1944 appointment). No realistic counter-hypothesis survives.
would_overturn: Discovery of a second A. B. Abayomi-Cole in Sierra Leone government service in the late 1940s (e.g., a relative in the Medical Department staff lists), or evidence that the biography's subject was outside Sierra Leone or not yet in the medical service during 1949-1951 (e.g., a record placing him at Durham University or in another colony in those years).
notes: Clean single-bio, single-stint case with a rare hyphenated surname (unique in corpus). The two deterministic FAILs are artifacts: position_sim fails only on abbreviation ('M.O.' vs 'Medical Officer'), and edition_cooccurrence fails because the biography first appears in 1956, after the stint's edition window — the bio's stated 1944-1955 M.O. tenure fully covers the 1949-1951 stint. Honour FAIL is expected: the O.B.E. was awarded in 1960, after the stint years.

arguments_against: The strongest counter-hypothesis is a second A. M. Abbott in British Guiana — a son, brother, or unrelated clerk — since 38 officials share this surname and 'Clerk' is a generic junior title; also the place_inherited check flags that most bio events inherit their colony from the first clause, and edition_cooccurrence mechanically failed. But the match does not rest on the generic 'Clerk' title or on inherited places: the bio explicitly opens 'Clerk to attorney-general, British Guiana, 1871' (place printed, not inherited), and states he became '3rd clerk, immigration department, Oct., 1876' — the staff list shows A. M. Abbott as Third Clerk in the Immigration Department in 1878-1879, an exact position+department+colony+date agreement on a specific office. He then moves to 'stipendiary magistrate's clerk, Mar., 1879', and the staff list correspondingly shifts him to Clerk, Judicial Establishment from 1880 onward, matching the career shape (magistrate's clerk, chief clerk to police magistrate and sheriff, acting magistracies are judicial-establishment posts). Both initials (A and M) match, not a single initial. The edition footprints also overlap heavily (1888, 1889, 1894, 1896, 1898, 1899 in both), contrary to what the mechanical check reported. For a hypothetical second A. M. Abbott to explain this, he would need the same two initials, same colony, same department, same year-range, and a career trajectory tracking the bio's stated promotions clause by clause — not realistic.
would_overturn: A staff list or biography revealing a second 'A. M. Abbott' serving in British Guiana in the 1870s-1890s (e.g., a son with the same initials in the same departments), or a record showing Arthur Moore Abbott left the colonial service or died before 1878 while the staff-list entries continued.
notes: The two printed bio versions are the same person (same entry, abbreviated vs expanded typesetting), not duplicate biographies. The mechanical edition_cooccurrence FAIL for the A. M. stint appears to be a checker artifact: bio editions [1888, 1889, 1890, 1894, 1896, 1898, 1899] and stint editions share at least six years. The bio's 'compiler of Summary Conviction Ordinances of the Col., 1887 to 1883' contains an OCR-garbled date range; it was not used as evidence. The staff list continuing to 1900 (beyond the bio's last edition, 1899) is consistent with the biography section simply dropping the entry while he remained in post.

arguments_against: Strongest case against: 38 Abbotts in the staff lists and 16 Abbott biographies exist, and both stints are flagged gate-compatible with two bios outside this case (abbott_t-kings-mill_e1837, abbott_x_b1914), so a different T. First, the match is not name-based: the biography's own printed text records the exact appointments the stints show — 'S.M.O., Sey., 1948' against a Seychelles staff list showing T. K. Abbott as Senior Medical Officer in 1949-1951 (S.M.O. is the standard abbreviation of Senior Medical Officer; the position_sim FAIL is an abbreviation artifact, score 16 for identical posts), and 'D.D.M.S., N. Borneo, 1951' against T. K. Abbott as Deputy Director of Medical Services, North Borneo, 1953-1954 (D.D.M.S. = Deputy Director of Medical Services, same artifact at score 21). Second, these are senior, single-incumbent specialist medical posts, not generic junior titles; two different T. K. Abbotts simultaneously tracing the same Seychelles-then-North-Borneo medical career is not a realistic hypothesis. Third, the two stints cohere with each other as one career arc exactly as the biography narrates it (Seychelles ends by 1951, the year the bio dates the N. Borneo appointment). The rival bio abbott_t-kings-mill_e1837 has a name ('T. Kings-mill') that is transparently an OCR/spelling variant of 'T. Kingsmill' and is almost certainly the same man duplicated, not a competitor; abbott_x_b1914 has no forename evidence and no claim to these specific posts. would_overturn: Evidence of a second contemporaneous T. K. Abbott in the colonial medical service — e.g. a staff list or biography showing two T. K. Abbotts listed simultaneously in different colonies in overlapping years, or a record showing the Seychelles S.M.O. or North Borneo D.D.M.S. of these years had a different forename (not Terence Kingsmill) or an incompatible birth year.

The edition_cooccurrence FAIL also looks mechanical: the bio appears in editions 1950, 1951, 1953, 1954, which overlap the Seychelles stint editions (1949-1951) and the North Borneo stint editions (1953-1954). Reviewer should check bio 'abbott_t-kings-mill_e1837' (outside this case): 'T. Kings-mill' is very likely an OCR variant of 'T. Kingsmill' and may be a duplicate record of this same person that should be merged at the biography level.

The only agreements are surname (the blocking key, not evidence) and a single-initial match 'H' ~ 'Hanafiah' on a single-initial biography flagged for the namesake trap, with 9 officials sharing this surname in the staff lists. No strong independent dimension exists; leaving unassigned.

No agreeing dimension beyond the fuzzy surname block ('ABERY' vs 'Abey'). The biography is a medical officer ('Assistant colonel surgeon, Ceylon, 1867', honours M.B.C.M.); the stint is a 'Stationmaster' in the 'Tasmanian Government Railways' — different profession, different colony (Ceylon vs Tasmania), no shared honour, no edition co-occurrence (bio appears only in 1897 as a 46-year-old career note; the stint spans 1894-1917 in a wholly different staff list). A career starting in Ceylon medicine in 1867 followed by Tasmanian railway station work into 1917 (50 years later) is implausible as one person.

Same person as the Tasmania stint records (identical name 'J. R. Abey', identical position 'Stationmaster', identical department 'Tasmanian Government Railways'), so the same objections apply: the biography is a Ceylon medical officer of 1867 with honours M.B.C.M.; this stint is a Tasmanian railway stationmaster listed 1913-1918, 46+ years later, with no colony, position, honour, or edition agreement. arguments_against: Strongest case against: (1) The stint is also gate-compatible with biography 'sabine_neville-warde_b1910' outside this case, so assigning it here could double-assign it. But that bio shares the identical forenames 'Neville Warde' and identical birth year 1910 with this one, and 'ABINE' is plainly an OCR truncation of 'SABINE' (the stint surname is 'Sabine'); this is a duplicate bio record of the same individual, not a rival identity, so the assignment is not a false merge across distinct people. (2) There are 7 Sabines in the staff lists and a 15-year gap (1934 Gold Coast to 1949 North Borneo) with zero overlapping editions, so the North Borneo officer could be a different N. W. Sabine. However, the match is not surname-plus-one-initial: both initials N.W. agree with 'Neville Warde', the age fits (39 at stint start), and crucially the career is in the same specialised cadre - the bio shows entry to the colonial audit dept ('col. dit dept' is OCR for 'col. audit dept') and 'ast. audr., Gold Coast, 1934', while the stint is 'Principal Auditor' in the AUDIT department, a natural 15-year promotion within the unified Colonial Audit Service whose officers routinely transferred between colonies. A second coincidental N. W. Sabine in the colonial audit branch is implausible. (3) 'Auditor' alone could be a recurring title, but 'Principal Auditor' is a senior, department-specific post, not a generic junior title. The counter-hypotheses are real enough (evidence gap, external duplicate bio) to withhold 'certain', but the identification is clearly more likely than not.
would_overturn: A staff list or biography expanding the North Borneo officer's initials to forenames other than Neville Warde; evidence that Neville Warde Sabine died, retired, or left the Colonial Audit Service before 1949; or a colony-agreeing record placing him in a different territory during 1949-1951.
notes: Biography 'abine_neville-warde_b1910' is an OCR-truncated duplicate of 'sabine_neville-warde_b1910' (leading 'S' lost; same forenames, same birth year 1910); the dossier notes the stint is gate-compatible with that external bio and 'already linked elsewhere or filtered'. The two bio records should be merged upstream. If the stint is already linked to the sabine_ record, this verdict corroborates that link rather than contesting it; the reviewer should resolve the bio-level deduplication, not the identity. The deterministic FAILs (colony, tenure_overlap, position_sim, edition_cooccurrence) all stem from the bio appearing only in the 1937 edition, 12 years before the stint; the audit-branch continuity is visible only in the verbatim text ('col. dit dept' = colonial audit dept).

But that is just the printed 'ditto' style of a single-colony career that begins 'Copyist, registrar's office, Br. Guiana, 1869', and the stint is in that same colony. V.), colony match (British Guiana), and two specific position-title matches at chronologically consistent dates (1874 bio promotion appearing in the 1877 staff list; 1882 bio promotion appearing by the 1883 staff list, the same year the printed name expands from 'E. Abraham' to 'E. A. V. Abraham'). The three deterministic FAILs (honour, edition_cooccurrence, place_inherited) are explained by printing conventions, not by conflicting evidence.

arguments_against: Strongest counter-case: a same-name relative (father or older brother, also 'H. A. Abrahams') serving in the Jamaica Constabulary in the 1920s-30s, with the biography's owner being a different person whose 1923-1939 career merely resembles it; the corpus has 10 officials and 3 biographies under this surname, and 'Assistant' and 'Clerk' are exactly the generic junior titles the instructions warn about. Also, the 1940 record reads 'Head Office' while the bio places the 1939 post in the 'med. dept.', and a male relative could share both initials. This fails because the match is not a single generic title but a three-step career trajectory reproduced with correct ordering and one-year listing lags: bio 'asst., constab., J'ca., 1923' vs staff list 'Assistant / Jamaica Constabulary' (1925, 1927); bio '2nd cl. clk., constab., 1933' vs '2nd Class Clerk / Jamaica Constabulary' (1934, 1937); bio '1st cl. clk., med. dept., 1939' vs '1st Class Clerk' (1940). For a relative to collide, he would need the same two initials AND the same posts in the same department in the same years as the bio self-reports, while the bio's owner is simultaneously absent from those very staff lists -- an unsupported double coincidence. The two stints are also continuous with each other (same name, same constabulary, promotion sequence), so they stand or fall together, and the age gate (entry at 17, listed at 19) is plausible for an assistant. The 'Head Office' vs 'med. dept.' discrepancy is a department-label difference in a single year, not a contradiction of person identity, and the 1940 listing drops to 'H. Abrahams' consistent with the same man.
would_overturn: Evidence of a second H. A. Abrahams in Jamaica government service in the 1920s-1930s (e.g., two H. A. Abrahams rows in the same edition's staff lists, or a relative's biography showing constabulary service in those years); or a record showing the 1940 'Head Office' 1st Class Clerk belonged to a department incompatible with the bio's medical-department post; or a death/departure record for the staff-list man before 1933 that breaks the promotion chain.
notes: Single-biography case. Both stints form one continuous Jamaica Constabulary-to-clerical career that the biography reproduces step-for-step with the expected one-year lag between appointment date and staff-list appearance (1923->1925, 1933->1934, 1939->1940). Deterministic position_sim FAIL on the 1925 stint is an abbreviation artifact ('asst., constab.' vs 'Assistant' in the Jamaica Constabulary department column). Edition co-occurrence is structurally absent: the biography appears only in 1956-1964 editions, long after the stint years, so its FAIL carries no negative weight.

Abramson' with a double initial, suggesting a possibly different man with a middle name; meanwhile the deterministic pass failed every colony/tenure/position/edition check, and the compiled timeline places the biography's later posts in Transjordan, not Palestine. Br. rep., Transjordan' clause, but the biography's surrounding career (mily. gov., Hebron, 1918; land commission and land department from 1920) is the Palestine administration, and Hebron sits in Palestine's southern district. Abramson in Palestine whose career independently tracks the biography through FOUR distinct, specific, senior posts in the same order at the right dates: chairman of the land commission (bio 1920 / staff list 1921), district governor of the southern district (bio 1922 / staff list 1923-24), district commissioner of the northern district (bio 1925 / staff list 1927-28), and commissioner of lands (bio 1927 / staff list 1929-32). The 1-2 year staff-list lag is the normal gap between appointment date and edition compilation. Corpus context shows exactly one official and one biography with this surname, so no live same-name candidate exists, and these are senior named offices, not generic junior titles. The 'A. A.' in 1921 is a one-off against eight subsequent years of 'A. Abramson' and is most plausibly a typesetting/OCR duplication.

Abramson') serving in the Palestine land administration in the 1920s; or evidence that Albert Abramson's 1920-1927 posts were held in Transjordan's separate establishment while a different Abramson held the identically-sequenced Palestine posts; or a death/retirement record for Albert Abramson before 1932 that contradicts the stint's later years.

The two-year offset between the bio's 'N. (3) 'Edgar' is the bio subject's second forename; Victorian/Edwardian officials commonly went by a middle name, and the initials check passes (E in [L, E]). The initials only partially agree ('V. G.' vs 'G.'), and with 96 officials and 31 biographies sharing the surname Cole, name-based inference is especially weak. Deterministic checks confirm: colony, tenure_overlap, position_sim, honour, and edition_cooccurrence all FAIL. The strongest case FOR the link — that 'G.' could be the same man printed with a dropped first initial who later moved to Kenya — fails because nothing in either record corroborates a move, and the career shape is discontinuous by eight years (1913-1921) across hemispheres.

Only initials and colony agree; every substantive deterministic dimension fails (no tenure overlap, no position comparison possible, no shared honours, no edition co-occurrence). The biography is a single 1888-edition entry for a junior 'Clerk for Immigration purposes' in 1881, with a 24-year undocumented gap before this 1905-1910 Magistrate stint -- 'clerk' is exactly the generic junior title the constraints flag as weak. Decisively, the dossier states the stint is also gate-compatible with a second biography outside this case, 'colenbrander_herman-james_b1863' -- an identically named Herman James Colenbrander (plausibly the same man, or a same-name relative). With two gate-compatible biographies sharing the full name and 4 biographies with this surname in the corpus, assigning the stint to e1881 risks a false merge that would be invisible later. No independent strong dimension (specific position match, honour, or multi-edition co-occurrence) supports the link, so the stint stays unassigned.

Bio is 'J. B.' while every staff-list record prints 'B. Colenbrander' — a partial initial match consistent with a different person (e.g. a brother) in a corpus with 3 same-surname officials and 4 same-surname biographies. The only positional echo is the generic junior title 'Clerk' ('Clerk to border agent' 1881 vs 'Clerk and Interpreter' 1894-97), explicitly weak evidence. Colony differs (Natal vs Zululand), there is a 13-year gap with no bridging record, and the bio appears only in the 1883 edition with no edition co-occurrence, tenure overlap, or shared honour. No strong independent dimension supports the merge.

Same partial-initial problem: bio 'J. B.' vs stint 'B.' This stint (1899-1910, rising to Civil Commissioner and Commissioner for Native Affairs, Zululand) is 18+ years after the bio's single 1881 event and shares only the colony Natal and the generic title 'Clerk' with it. It is plausibly continuous with the Zululand 1894-97 stint (same name, same 'Clerk and Interpreter' post in 'Resident Magistrates', Zululand annexed to Natal in 1897), i.e. the two stints more likely form one 'B. Colenbrander' career that need not be the 'J. B.' of the 1883 biography. No tenure overlap, no edition co-occurrence, no honours. Insufficient evidence to merge.

arguments_against: Strongest counter-case: this is a single-initial biography, so the stint could belong to a same-name relative (a son or brother, also 'M. Covington') serving in Ceylon's Medical Department in 1877-1883, or to a successor holding the same generic-sounding post. That hypothesis fails on several grounds. First, the corpus contains exactly one official and one biography with this surname, so no second Covington is attested anywhere in the staff lists or biographical sections. Second, the match is not on a generic junior title but on a specific named office: the bio's career culminates in 'assistant colonial surgeon' (1859, 1st class 1867) in Ceylon, and the stint is 'Assistant Colonial Surgeons' in Ceylon's Medical Department -- the exact office, exact colony, exact initial. Third, the 1883 edition prints both the biography and the staff listing simultaneously, i.e. the compilers of that very volume treated the biographical subject as the then-current Assistant Colonial Surgeon; a Record of Services entry with no death/retirement note alongside a current staff listing of the same name and post is the editors' own identification. Fourth, the career shape is continuous: a man promoted to assistant colonial surgeon 1st class in 1867 still being listed as Assistant Colonial Surgeon in 1877-1883 is the natural continuation, and his age (entered service 1849, so roughly 50s-60s by 1883) is unremarkable for a serving colonial surgeon. The deterministic FAILs are mechanical artifacts: tenure_overlap and position_sim fail only because the bio's placed events stop at 1867, before the stint window opens, and edition_cooccurrence fails despite both records demonstrably appearing in the 1883 edition.

notes: All five deterministic similarity checks failed mechanically, but each failure is explained by the bio's compiled timeline ending in 1867 (no placed events overlap the 1877-1883 stint window) rather than by any contradiction. The verbatim texts agree on colony (Ceylon), department/profession (medical), specific office (assistant colonial surgeon), initial (M.), and 1883 edition co-occurrence, and the surname is unique in the corpus. The edition_cooccurrence FAIL appears to be a tooling artifact worth investigating: bio editions [1883] and stint editions [1877, 1878, 1879, 1880, 1883] plainly share 1883.

arguments_against: The strongest case against: the biography's last printed event is 1866, and the stint begins 1879 — a 13-year gap. A son or other relative named J. Craw could have succeeded James in the same Natal office, and colonial offices did pass between kin; the staff list prints only the initial 'J', so it cannot distinguish James from, say, John. The place evidence is also weak in isolation: the 1866 event inherits 'Natal' from the previous clause rather than stating it. Why this fails: there is no gap in the post itself to suggest succession — the bio puts James as 'first clerk in the registry of deeds and stamp office' from 1866, and the staff list shows a J. Craw holding exactly that rank ('1st Clerk') in exactly that department ('Registrar of Deeds') in the same colony continuously through 1879-1883, the natural continuation of a single tenure. The corpus contains exactly one Craw official and one Craw biography, so no second candidate exists; and the 1883 edition prints the biography and the staff-list entry concurrently, which fits one living serving officer. The succession hypothesis requires inventing an undocumented second Craw who happens to hold the identical specific post; nothing in the dossier supports him. Still, because the link rests on a single-initial staff name plus position continuity across a 13-year listing gap, a realistic (if unevidenced) relative-successor hypothesis survives, so this stops short of certain.

The biography describes a junior field officer in the East Africa Protectorate ('Asst. jun. staff survr., E.A.P., June, 1914; asst. dist. consnr., Dec., 1913'), while this stint is a senior London-based company director in North Borneo ('Court of Directors', rising to 'Vice-Chairman' by 1913 and 'Vice-President' by 1920). Every deterministic dimension fails (colony, tenure_overlap, position_sim, honour, edition_cooccurrence); only surname and a single initial 'E' agree, and the bio uses initials 'E. J.' vs the stint's full forename 'Edward'. With 11 officials and 5 biographies sharing this surname in the corpus, name agreement carries no weight.

arguments_against: Strongest case against: (1) the colony check FAILS — the biography places him on the 'Uganda rly.' while the stint is under Kenya, so these could be two different railway officers in two different territories; (2) 'Assistant Traffic Manager' is a title plural in the staff list ('Assistant Traffic Managers'), implying several simultaneous holders, so a same-name relative (brother/cousin) in the same trade could hold the Kenya post; (3) the corpus holds 2 biographies with this surname, so the stint might belong to the other Dover. Why these fail: the Uganda Railway, despite its name, ran from Mombasa through what became Kenya Colony and was headquartered and staffed under the East Africa Protectorate — its officers appear in the Kenya/East Africa staff lists, so the Uganda-vs-Kenya 'mismatch' is an administrative-geography artifact, not a contradiction; the position match is near-verbatim and specific ('Asst. traffic man.' vs 'Assistant Traffic Managers' in the Traffic department), not a generic junior title; the initials H. B. match exactly on two initials; chronology is a clean fit (appointed 1913, listed 1915 and 1917); and the corpus contains only ONE official with this surname in the staff lists, so there is no competing stint-holder, and the second Dover biography is not entangled here (its initials evidently did not match H. B.). No realistic counter-hypothesis survives.
would_overturn: Evidence of a second H. B. Dover in East Africa railway service in 1913-1917 (e.g. a staff-list entry distinguishing two Dovers in the Traffic department, or the second Dover biography turning out to also carry initials H. B. with a railway career); or evidence that the biography's subject left the Uganda Railway before 1915 for a different territory, making the 1915-1917 Kenya listing someone else's.
notes: The deterministic colony/tenure/position failures are all downstream of one labeling artifact: the Uganda Railway was administered from and largely located in the East Africa Protectorate (later Kenya Colony), so its staff appear under Kenya in the staff lists while the biography names the railway, not the territory. Once that is recognized, position (assistant traffic manager, Traffic dept.), initials (H. B., two initials), and chronology (1913 appointment preceding 1915-1917 listings; bio and stint co-occur in the 1917 edition) all agree, and the corpus contains only one staff-list official with this surname.
